title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several routine items and made key decisions including awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding, and establishing a $100/day fine policy for illegal sanitary sewer discharges. The committee also approved construction agreements for multiple road projects totaling over $200,000 in consulting and utility costs.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Water and sewer connection agreement - Radkkey Avenue

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scoffield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project may be moved up to 2028. Staff noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion included updates on Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading, Scoffield Avenue concrete repairs by HOS Construction, and Fuller Street restoration by Melvin. A special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue was tentatively scheduled for Thursday, May 28 at 6:00 PM.

Staff reported road limits were removed April 29 along with Marathon County. Crews completed mailbox repairs, sign repairs, and spring curb sweeping. Dewatering operations were conducted near Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week due to flooding. Staff addressed storm damage from an event near East Nick affecting trees. Culvert failures on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch culvert. Vehicle maintenance included removing plow equipment and preparing summer equipment. A new MAC tandem axle chassis has been ordered.

Josh reported a service repair at Caribou Coffee and continued well maintenance. Sanitary lift stations experienced significant activity due to clear water infiltration. Data showed April treated 53-54 million gallons while only 37 million gallons were pumped into the distribution system, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff identified sump pump discharges into sanitary sewers as a contributing factor. In-pipe technologies will conduct an INI study to identify infiltration sources. Well 2 rehab at Carrie started the day of the meeting.

Michael presented the annual report showing operating revenues resulting in net income of approximately $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was reported at 6%. Utility value reached nearly $34 million, an increase of 6.7% from infrastructure placed in service including Wells 7 and 8. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total: 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in other areas. Cash reserves total $6.8 million. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions at the state level. He noted while grants help with one-time costs, recurring annual funding for street maintenance and snow plowing remains inadequate, requiring subsidies from general tax dollars. Wisconsin remains affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership costs. Motion to adopt passed unanimously.

Michael reported King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at the Ryan Street site, with an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Analysis showed the village saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, resulting in approximately $130,000 savings across 2026 projects. Motion to award contract to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on fining residents illegally discharging sump pump water into sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. After discussion about waiting for the in-pipe technology study results to identify violators, the committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines not to exceed $100/day for first offenses. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a builder wants to construct a duplex on lot 1 at 2230 Radkkey Avenue where water/sewer mains currently dead end. Staff proposed allowing a temporary long lateral connection until Radkkey Avenue is reconstructed. Committee requested adding a deadline, resulting in language requiring reconstruction no later than end of 2029 construction season. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share covers utility costs of $94,860 ($6,156 sanitary sewer, $88,704 water main). Steam Construction is the contractor. The water main requires relocation due to storm sewer conflicts. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910; staff recommended MSA. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730; staff recommended Clark Dietz due to using full-time experienced staff versus MSA's summer intern. Committee approved MSA for roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel. Motion passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Village Board to adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines not to exceed $100/day
Authorize agreement with 2230 Radkkey Avenue property owner for temporary utility connection with assessment due when street is reconstructed no later than end of 2029
Approve intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction ($94,860 Weston share)
Award construction services to MSA for Alderson/Jelnik roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue project
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration impact at next meeting
Consider adding street lighting to Scoffield Avenue between Birch Street and Camp Phillips Road during future reconstruction

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission also heard that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary unanimously.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved unanimously with a motion and second by Renee.

discussions.item: Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that the competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. A commissioner questioned room tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission approved a $5,000 grant (same as last year) unanimously, with direction to provide better room usage data from hotels in their post-event report.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, describing meeting the candidates and noting that Anastasia won the competition. She emphasized that the candidates were impressed with what Marathon County had to offer and this was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow Balloonfest directed to obtain and report accurate hotel room usage data from hotels in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. The meeting included discussion about building orientation challenges due to a gas pipeline and future expansion capabilities for the police facility.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Unfinished business - Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Future agenda items and staff updates

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court Facility Site Plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to face east due to a 36-inch gas pipeline running through the property, which reduced fill costs significantly. The building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth (currently 40 officers and 4 clerical staff). Discussion included the alternate storage shed placement, with commissioners suggesting moving it north to allow for future building expansion. The landscaping plan meets current relaxed code standards. Motion to approve was made and passed unanimously with conditions 2-5 as listed in staff recommendations.

discussions.item: Salt Storage Shed Inspection Program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff proposed a new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per salt storage facility in wellhead protection areas - one warm weather inspection for structural integrity and one winter operational inspection. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT requirements. Staff noted one existing facility near the Sternberg well that never went through the wellhead protection conditional use process and should be contacted about compliance. Commissioner Lumper praised the well-organized program. Motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented was made, seconded, and passed unanimously with no discussion.

Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send calendar invites. Discussion arose about potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with staff noting Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with commissioners or incorporated into the comprehensive plan process.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via video conference. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing previous flexibility shown with the Greenheck Turner building to avoid excessive taxpayer costs. He also requested that the salt storage shed inspection program include annual reporting to track compliance visits at the limited number of sites in wellhead protection zones.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-yearly inspections
Staff to report inspection program findings to Plan Commission and Public Works committee next year
Staff to contact existing salt storage facility near Sternberg well about wellhead protection compliance
Joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes and received comprehensive overviews of each department's mission, structure, budget, and current initiatives.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations functions required by statute - overview of agenda structure
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Department presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Administrator Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction under section 2.04 sub5, including oversight of Veteran Services, Social Services, Health Department, North Central Health Care, and the Aging Disability Resource Center. He detailed reporting relationships with statutory bodies including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the committee will focus on developing a new strategic plan by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission and four divisions: environmental health (over 1,200 licenses), nursing/communicable disease, community health improvement, and administration. She highlighted the Nurse Family Partnership program graduating its first two-year-olds, a new homeless system response position funded by the county and city of Wausau, public health vending machines with free tick kits, and emergency preparedness capabilities. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE with about $5 million budget, roughly half from tax levy.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of absent Director Christa, describing the department's 140 FTE across child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support divisions. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program, noting housing instability as a growing concern. The department's 2026 budget is over $22 million with approximately $7 million in tax levy. Leonard indicated future requests for restructuring within units due to increased need.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported the department increased to 3.0 FTE in 2025. Customer interactions jumped from 5,900 to 8,149 in 2025. Veterans received $44 million annually in benefits and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo highlighted initiatives including surviving spouse benefits under the PACT Act, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts (noting 30 suicides in Marathon County in 2025, 7 being veterans). He is exploring the Staff Sergeant Gordon Fox VA suicide prevention grant.

Executive Director Mike Ray described the four-county regional organization serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with approximately 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. He detailed services including information assistance, Meals on Wheels, Medicare/Medicaid benefits specialists, dementia care, and falls prevention. Ray noted significant financial challenges with nutrition program costs rising 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat. Marathon County's 2026 contribution is $413,135. The ADRC had not requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before budget process
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan items to future meetings for committee input
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as initially stated)
Committee members encouraged to email agenda item requests to Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increases to park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made committee appointments.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledge reports from committees and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing - Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 per single family unit to $761, from $250 to $563 for duplex units, and to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning the justification for the numbers without detailed operational data. Trustee Clark supported the increase noting population growth requires more park infrastructure. Ordinance 26-010 passed unanimously with motion by Lope Sorrell and second by Zagami. Resolution 2026-015 also passed unanimously with motion by Hermling and second by Clark.

The board debated amendments to section 1.08 regarding virtual attendance. Trustees discussed removing specific requirements about health, safety, and welfare justifications, preferring broader language. Trustee Beck moved to amend striking sentences requiring justification reasons, seconded by Clark. The amendment passed with two opposed (Hermling and Clark). The original motion as amended also passed with Hermling and Clark voting no. The final policy requires 12-hour notice, camera on, and prohibits participation in closed session.

Chief Finkey explained this creates reciprocal fire inspection recognition among Safer, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, and Riverside. Once one department inspects a food truck and applies a sticker, other communities accept that inspection for the season, reducing redundant inspections and costs for vendors. Passed unanimously with motion by Lopes Sorrell and second by Zagami.

Board discussed challenges with the new Kennedy Park location after losing the Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue site to development. Concerns raised about parking on grass along Alderson Street and potential field damage. Trustee Beck raised cost concerns but staff confirmed the market operates at a surplus through vendor fees, grants, and token reimbursements. The market grew from 25 stalls at the original location to 53 currently. No action taken; discussion only item with possible future location changes if current site proves unworkable.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes Sorrell, passed unanimously. Board agreed to include committee/commission applications in future packets for transparency.

Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street approved for secondhand article and jewelry dealer licenses. Motion amended by Beck, second by Clark, to require satisfactory background check completion and occupancy permit before license issuance. Passed unanimously as amended.

Public Works Director Michael reported conducting speed studies showing 85th percentile at 45 mph. DOT engineering study classified it as 35 mph road, but village engineer may sign off on higher limit pending additional data collection. Will return to board for action. Trustee Zagami noted the speed has been inconsistent with ordinance for over two months.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants ($108,000) when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He also criticized the appointment process for committees, noting Lisa Beck was introduced at a SAFER meeting before official board appointment. He requested a detailed breakdown of Kennedy Park project funding and timeline, and questioned justification for the proposed park impact fee increases without operational cost data.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased via Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor license ordinance (26-008) approved creating reciprocal inspection agreement
Ordinance 26-009 amending committee structure approved
Nuisance ordinance (26-011) approved
E-bike ordinance (26-012) amending traffic and vehicles chapter approved
Resolution 2026-014 supporting sustainable transportation funding approved
Remote meeting attendance policy amended - strikes justification requirements, requires notice, camera on, no closed session participation
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC approved for secondhand dealer licenses pending background check and occupancy
Compliance assurance plan approved
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Staff to complete additional Weston Avenue speed study and return to board
Staff to prepare intergovernmental agreement for Ringle building inspection assistance
Committee applications to be included in future board packets

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to at-large executive committee positions, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that impacted the Ringle area. The board also approved budget amendments for social services and out-of-home placement reserves.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of meeting notice and roll call
Committee appointments presentation by Chair Gibbs
Election of at-large executive committee members

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diverse perspectives, developing new supervisors as leaders, and honoring supervisor preferences. Over 34% of the board is in their first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained she nominated Leur because she 'advocates for our county's smallest children and our oldest elders.' Paper ballots were used and Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.

Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that impacted Ringle and other areas. The state waived $13.10 per ton, Ringle planned to waive $2.33, and the county proposed reducing fees to $36.94 per ton (commercial rate minus waivers) to cover operational costs. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special dispensation to speak and requested the county waive fees entirely for affected residents. Supervisor Robinson suggested tracking costs and potentially reimbursing the community foundation if needed. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to bring the resolution forward without committee review, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously.
Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but was not unanimous.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to address the board regarding the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty spoke about the community's self-reliance in cleanup efforts, noting businesses have donated up to $40,000 in hauling expenses, and requested the county waive fees entirely for six months. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents with demolished homes, noting 44 homes affected and that many insurance adjusters haven't yet arrived, requesting more time and relief from Marathon County.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 24-26 approved for out-of-home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved for social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to approximately $36.94 per ton for disaster-related debris through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to pursue discussions with community foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid waste management board directed to develop long-term policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Supervisor Gao requested notification of future solid waste board meetings

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems (not other zoning or pouts fees) for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Rezoning petition 5A1: Tim Veland on behalf of James Offer and David Trait - Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley
Rezoning petition 5A2: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz - Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried with no opposition.

discussions.item: Rezoning petition 5A1: James Offer and David Trait property
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential in the Town of Green Valley. The Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Rezoning petition 5A2: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in the Town of McMillan. Supervisor Lever asked about the conservancy nature; Pogle explained it currently has a home and horse riding stable, and the rezone would make the non-conforming home a conforming use. The Town Board recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather damage
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the tornado in Ringle, Kronenwetter, Easton, and Weston areas. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck proposed an amendment to only waive reconnect fees for septic systems ($160), not other fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor K and passed. The amended resolution to forward to county board also passed, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagen Busher provided an overview of the department's operations, explaining the landfill accepts about 1,000 tons daily, partnerships with Portage and Shawano counties, the renewable natural gas project with Vidi Energy that came online in September 2025, and upcoming challenges including a $6 million cell construction project and required leachate treatment by 2032 due to PFAS concerns.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie. He noted the department operates both county and city park systems since 1924, manages 17 county parks and approximately 30,700 acres of county forest. Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Lovelin announced his retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

actionItems: Forward amended resolution to county board waiving only septic system reconnect fees ($160) for properties damaged by April 17, 2026 tornado
Rezoning for James Offer/David Trait property forwarded to county board for final approval
Rezoning for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property forwarded to county board for final approval
Solid Waste to reapply for clean water fund loan for leachate treatment facility by October 2025 for 2027 funding cycle
Joe Tucker selected as new County Forest Administrator following Tom Lovelin's retirement

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved installation of new kayak launch signs for the Eau Claire River Trail and deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until the next meeting. The committee also heard updates on Kennedy Park fundraising efforts and discussed graffiti removal challenges on the Oaklair River Trail.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from April 27, 2026 meeting
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail discussion
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the minutes from the previous meeting. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Director Sean Oster provided a timeline of the Kennedy Park master plan process from 2023 to present, including the recent ribbon cutting on May 9th. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about funding responsibilities. Oster explained the village planned to contribute approximately $7 million for infrastructure while the Friends of Kennedy Park fundraises for other improvements. The Friends group currently has approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation toward a $117,000 goal for field lights and plans to make a public check presentation at their June tournament. A committee member emphasized that Friends of Kennedy Park are volunteers who deserve grace, and that master plans are guides subject to change over time.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and a PDF showing the approximate location of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment use. Dan showed photos of mat installation and explained the work done with TC Energy (formerly ANR pipeline) oversight.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the next meeting because Chair Katrina, who requested the item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy had come specifically to oppose allowing dogs at the public pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' His objection was noted for the record.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Oster explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete due to limited access for pressure washing equipment. The bridges cannot support larger equipment needed. Staff discussed potential solutions including anti-vandalism coatings or sealers. Committee members suggested volunteer opportunities for trail maintenance, noting that previous improvements were accomplished with volunteer help from directors on a team building day. No formal action was taken; staff will explore cleaning options.

Dan Bahnub from Marathon County Parks Foundation presented version 7 of kayak launch signs for the Eau Claire River water trail. Signs will be installed at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks landings in Weston, with additional signs in Schofield. The signs include maps, history, QR codes for river gauge data, and donation information. Funding comes from Marathon County Parks Foundation grants. Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve installation of the signs passed unanimously.

Staff raised a request from an individual asking about placing food allergy awareness signage on playgrounds. The committee agreed to bring this back for further discussion, though members expressed mixed feelings about setting precedent for such requests.

publicComment: Two members of the public commented. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) opposed allowing dogs at the aquatic center, stating dogs should swim in rivers and lakes, not public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), speaking via the online name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned the Kennedy Park funding report, calling the $8,700 figure 'too coincidental' and asking where grant money and glow event proceeds were, suggesting the Friends of Kennedy Park were not being transparent about finances.
actionItems: Approved installation of kayak launch signs at Yellow Banks, Ross Avenue, and Highway J landings - motion passed unanimously
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to explore graffiti removal and cleaning options for Oaklair River Trail
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be brought back for future discussion
Kickoff meeting for Mock Miller Park master plan scheduled for next Tuesday with Rettler
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged budget reports for April. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, elections, licensing, and budgets. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF one and TIF two detail reports for April
Educational presentations and reports - Municipal Clerk's budget presentation
Staff remarks and farmers market update

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: TIF one and TIF two detail reports
discussions.body: Discussion included an update on TIF closure timeline. Staff indicated they are anticipating October/November for final bond payoff, with bonds due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, expected this summer. Staff confirmed the numbers still look good and they will be able to pay off the debt.

discussions.item: Acknowledgment of work product transmittals (items 5, 6, and 7)
discussions.body: Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. The motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented an extensive overview of clerk's office operations covering elections, licensing, tax collection, and pet licensing. She noted the office went from four full-time staff to currently two full-time and one part-time employee. The election budget is higher in even years due to presidential/gubernatorial elections, with postage jumping from $6,000 to $15,000 for 2024. She discussed replacing Badger Books at approximately $11,000 for four units. Pet licensing was brought back in-house from Pet Data, saving approximately $5,495 annually. Discussion included the increase in pet licensing fees from $10 to $20 for neutered animals due to Marathon County raising fees to cover Humane Society costs. The quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well, with vendors happy with the setup and location. She noted some challenges with weather on Tuesdays causing vendors to leave early. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft vendors, and produce sellers. Discussion included parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with clarification that only the west side of Alderson allows parking while the east side remains no parking.

Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though still in the metro area. This was his last meeting. The chair noted there are now multiple open spots on this and other committees and encouraged outreach to interested citizens.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution this summer to call TIF bonds for October payoff
Staff to contact Ellers and notify US Bank associate trust regarding bond payoff
Seek citizens to fill open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to nuisance ordinances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with restrictions on emotos, and authorized the razing of an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring both items for further staff work.

agenda.item: Call to order, Pledge of Allegiance, and roll call
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendments

discussions.body: Jim Senalt addressed the committee with concerns about the new farmers market location, stating it was a 'terrible execution' and predicting the park's grassy areas would be destroyed. He questioned ADA-compliant handicap parking placement on grass rather than asphalt, asked about the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks, and criticized the age of some code violations that have been pending for years without visible progress.

Minutes from the previous meeting were approved unanimously on a motion by Olsen, seconded by Shawanka. The committee acknowledged reports from Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, and Police. Staff noted 3020 Mountain View Avenue will come off the code enforcement roll after sale and permits filed. Chief Hunt reported 2,394 police calls in March and announced the department has hired its 40th officer. Motion to acknowledge reports passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments to Chapter 50, including previously discussed changes to tree limb height requirements over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet specifications were switched). Tolson confirmed no additional changes were needed. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion occurred regarding emotos (electric mini dirt bikes). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos from roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes and citing safety concerns including wheelies past pedestrians. The committee debated whether to also restrict electric scooters, ultimately deciding to leave officer discretion for low-speed children's scooters. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Shawanka, to recommend approval with emotos prohibited from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously.

Tolson initiated discussion noting Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (typically 5-hour windows). Public Works Director Michael stated plowing rarely occurs before 4-5am and suggested eliminating the overnight restriction except for signing 6-8 problem areas. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight parking, preferring uniformity with other municipalities to give officers time to clear vehicles before plows arrive. The committee decided to defer the item until July, with Michael to prepare a list of specific problem areas requiring signed restrictions.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest school parking lot. Committee members questioned necessity given no reported accidents and existing nearby crossings. Staff estimated a basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000, while a lighted system would be $25,000-30,000. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Shawanka, to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering installation costs passed unanimously. Michael will contact the church to determine willingness to pay.

Chief Hunt presented a regional fire inspection program involving Stevens Point, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Plover, Rib Mountain, and SAFER. One inspection and sticker would be valid across all participating municipalities. Fire Inspector Marty already failed one vendor due to improper galvanized steel LP piping and unsecured propane tanks. Wausau opted out to maintain their own inspection program. Motion by Shawanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections needed for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicowski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue for a new duplex on a private drive. Chief Hunt also identified Weston Villa Apartments on Delicowski as needing review (eight apartments with eight separate addresses plus a utility room address). Motion passed unanimously to recommend board approval.

Staff requested authorization to begin the raze order process for an abandoned structure. The property has been abandoned since 2010-2011 and was previously considered for a fire department training burn but deemed too unsafe. Owners had housed chickens inside the structure. Property taxes are currently paid; demolition costs will be added to the tax roll if unpaid. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend board authorization passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment, criticizing the new farmers market location as poorly executed and predicting damage to park grass areas. He questioned handicap parking placement on grass rather than asphalt for ADA compliance, asked about the rationale for new fire inspections on food trucks, raised concerns about food truck regulations on public vs private property, and criticized the age of some pending code violations.
actionItems: Chapter 50 nuisance amendments recommended to Board of Trustees for approval
E-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property recommended to Board of Trustees
Michael to prepare list of specific areas requiring signed parking restrictions for July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding willingness to pay for crosswalk installation
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved
Address corrections for E911 compliance recommended to Board of Trustees
Raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue authorized
Staff to investigate property concern at Sternberg and South Timber
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
